title: Rhubarb custard cake
subtitle: A buttermilk sponge baked over a layer of rhubarb with a custard-like interior
excerpt: Forced rhubarb pressed into a deep tin with a vanilla buttermilk batter poured over: as it bakes the rhubarb sinks slightly and the batter sets around it into a moist, custard-edged crumb. Serves warm with cream.
sourceNotes: Rhubarb cakes with a custard-style texture are documented in British baking from the early twentieth century, drawing on the tradition of baked fruit puddings with a set egg-rich batter. Forced rhubarb, grown in the dark to produce tender pink stalks, has been a British commercial crop since the Victorian era. Florence White (Good Things in England, 1932) includes several rhubarb pudding and baked-cake recipes. The recipe here follows the classic British approach.

glossaryTerms.definition: Rhubarb grown in the dark in heated sheds, which produces slender, tender, bright-pink stalks with a milder, less fibrous flavour than outdoor field rhubarb. Available from January to March. The pale, delicate stalks soften quickly in baking.

content.text: The rhubarb goes into the tin first, arranged in a tight layer and scattered with sugar. The buttermilk batter poured over sets around the rhubarb rather than enclosing it in a thick crumb, producing the characteristic custard-edged texture near the fruit.
works best here; the stalks are thinner, more tender, and a better colour than outdoor rhubarb.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Grease and line the base and sides of a 23 cm square tin. Arrange the rhubarb pieces in a snug single layer on the base of the tin and scatter the 50 g caster sugar evenly over them. Set aside while you make the batter.

Beat the butter and 175 g caster sugar together until pale and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each. Fold in the flour, baking powder, and bicarbonate of soda in alternation with the buttermilk, starting and ending with the flour. Add the vanilla.

Spoon the batter over the rhubarb layer and spread gently to cover. Bake for 50 to 55 minutes until the top is deep golden and a skewer inserted in the centre of the batter (away from any visible rhubarb) comes out clean. The edges will be set while the centre remains slightly softer — this is the custard-like quality the recipe is after, not under-baking. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes before turning out or cutting directly from the tin.
